audioscript.text: Section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a telephone conversation between a woman who is looking for a job and a man from a company that organizes cycling holidays.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Test, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 6.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will see that there is an example that has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only, the conversation relating to this will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello, Pembroke Cycling Holidays, Bob speaking.
audioscript.speaker: BOB

audioscript.text: Oh, hello.
audioscript.speaker: MARGARET

audioscript.text: I've seen your advert for people to lead cycle trips.
audioscript.speaker: MARGARET

audioscript.text: Are you the right person to speak to?
audioscript.speaker: MARGARET

audioscript.text: Yes, I am.
audioscript.speaker: BOB

audioscript.text: Could I have your name please?
audioscript.speaker: BOB

audioscript.text: It's Margaret Smith.
audioscript.speaker: MARGARET

audioscript.text: The woman's surname is Smith.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So, Smith has been written in the space.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen, because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 6.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello, Pembroke Cycling Holidays, Bob speaking.
audioscript.speaker: BOB

audioscript.text: Oh, hello.
audioscript.speaker: MARGARET

audioscript.text: I've seen your advert for people to lead cycle trips.
audioscript.speaker: MARGARET

audioscript.text: Are you the right person to speak to?
audioscript.speaker: MARGARET

audioscript.text: Yes, I am.
audioscript.speaker: BOB

audioscript.text: Could I have your name please?
audioscript.speaker: BOB

audioscript.text: It's Margaret Smith.
audioscript.speaker: MARGARET

audioscript.text: Are you looking for a permanent job, Margaret?
audioscript.speaker: BOB

audioscript.text: No, temporary.
audioscript.speaker: MARGARET

audioscript.text: I've got a permanent job starting in a few months' time.
audioscript.speaker: MARGARET

audioscript.text: And I want to do something else until then.
audioscript.speaker: MARGARET

audioscript.text: What work do you do?
audioscript.speaker: BOB

audioscript.text: This will probably sound crazy.
audioscript.speaker: MARGARET

audioscript.text: I used to be a lawyer, and then I made a complete career change, and I'm going to be a doctor.
audioscript.speaker: MARGARET

audioscript.text: I've just finished my training.
audioscript.speaker: MARGARET

audioscript.text: Right, and have you had any experience of leading cycle trips?
audioscript.speaker: BOB

audioscript.text: Yes, I've led several bike tours in Africa.
audioscript.speaker: MARGARET

audioscript.text: The trip to India that I had arranged to lead next month has now been canceled.
audioscript.speaker: MARGARET

audioscript.text: So when I saw you were advertising for tour leaders, I decided to apply.
audioscript.speaker: MARGARET

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: BOB

audioscript.text: Now we normally have 2 or 3 leaders on a trip, depending on the size of the group.
audioscript.speaker: BOB

audioscript.text: Some tours are for very experienced cyclists, but we've got a tour coming up soon in Spain.
audioscript.speaker: BOB

audioscript.text: Which is proving so popular, we need an additional leader.
audioscript.speaker: BOB

audioscript.text: It's a cycling holiday for families, would that suit you?
audioscript.speaker: BOB

audioscript.text: It certainly would.
audioscript.speaker: MARGARET

audioscript.text: I enjoy working with children.
audioscript.speaker: MARGARET

audioscript.text: And I probably need some more experience before I go on a really challenging trip.
audioscript.speaker: MARGARET

audioscript.text: That tour includes several teenagers.
audioscript.speaker: BOB

audioscript.text: Have you worked with that age group before?
audioscript.speaker: BOB

audioscript.text: Yes, I'm a volunteer worker in a youth club, where I help people to improve their cycling skills.
audioscript.speaker: MARGARET

audioscript.text: Before that, I helped out in a cycling club where I taught beginners.
audioscript.speaker: MARGARET

audioscript.text: Well, that's great.
audioscript.speaker: BOB

audioscript.text: Now the trip I mentioned is just for a fortnight, but there might be the possibility of leading other tours after that.
audioscript.speaker: BOB

audioscript.text: Would that fit in with your plans?
audioscript.speaker: BOB

audioscript.text: That would be fine.
audioscript.speaker: MARGARET

audioscript.text: I'll be free for five months.
audioscript.speaker: MARGARET

audioscript.text: My job is due to start on October the second, and I'm available from May the 1st until late September.
audioscript.speaker: MARGARET

audioscript.text: Good, now is there anything I need to know about the food you eat?
audioscript.speaker: BOB

audioscript.text: We usually have one or two people in the group who don't eat meat, or have some sort of food allergy.
audioscript.speaker: BOB

audioscript.text: So we're always very careful about that.
audioscript.speaker: BOB

audioscript.text: Yes, I'm allergic to cheese.
audioscript.speaker: MARGARET

audioscript.text: Would that be a problem?
audioscript.speaker: MARGARET

audioscript.text: No, as long as we have enough notice, we can deal with that.
audioscript.speaker: BOB

audioscript.text: That's great.
audioscript.speaker: MARGARET

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 7 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 7 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: It sounds really interesting.
audioscript.speaker: MARGARET

audioscript.text: Would you like me to fill in an application form?
audioscript.speaker: MARGARET

audioscript.text: Yes, please.
audioscript.speaker: BOB

audioscript.text: Where should I post it to?
audioscript.speaker: BOB

audioscript.text: Could you send it to 27 Arbuthnot Place - ARBUTHNOT - Place, Dumfries.
audioscript.speaker: MARGARET

audioscript.text: And what's the postcode please?
audioscript.speaker: BOB

audioscript.text: DG74PH.
audioscript.speaker: MARGARET

audioscript.text: Was that P papa or B bravo?
audioscript.speaker: BOB

audioscript.text: P papa.
audioscript.speaker: MARGARET

audioscript.text: Got that, if you could return the application form by Friday this week, we can interview you on Tuesday next week, say 2:30 would that be possible for you?
audioscript.speaker: BOB

audioscript.text: Yes, it's fine.
audioscript.speaker: MARGARET

audioscript.text: You're quite a long way from where I live, so I'll drive over on Monday.
audioscript.speaker: MARGARET

audioscript.text: Should I bring anything to the interview?
audioscript.speaker: MARGARET

audioscript.text: We'll have your application form of course.
audioscript.speaker: BOB

audioscript.text: But we'll need to see any certificates you've got that are relevant in cycling, first aid or whatever.
audioscript.speaker: BOB

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: MARGARET

audioscript.text: And at the interview, we'd like to find out about your experience of being a tour guide.
audioscript.speaker: BOB

audioscript.text: So, could you prepare a 10 minute talk about that please?
audioscript.speaker: BOB

audioscript.text: You don't need slides or any complicated equipment, just some notes.
audioscript.speaker: BOB

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: MARGARET

audioscript.text: I'll start thinking about that straight away.
audioscript.speaker: MARGARET

audioscript.text: Good.
audioscript.speaker: BOB

audioscript.text: Well, we'll look forward to receiving your application form, and we'll contact you to confirm the interview.
audioscript.speaker: BOB

audioscript.text: Thanks very much.
audioscript.speaker: MARGARET

audioscript.text: Thank you, Margaret.
audioscript.speaker: BOB

audioscript.text: Goodbye.
audioscript.speaker: BOB

audioscript.text: Bye.
audioscript.speaker: MARGARET

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: Interview:

audioscript.text: Section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a podcast about an area called the Sheepmarket in a New Zealand city.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 14.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 14.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Welcome to this podcast about the Sheepmarket, which is one of the oldest parts of the city.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As its name suggests, there was originally a market here where farmers brought their sheep.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But now it's been redeveloped into a buzzing vibrant area of the city, which is also home to one of the city's fastest growing communities.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The nearby university has always meant the area's popular with students who come in to enjoy the lively nightlife.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But now graduates embarking on careers in the world of fashion and design are buying up the new apartments recently built here to replace the small houses where the market workers used to live.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The narrow old side streets are great places for finding original pictures, jewelry, and ceramics which won't break the bank, as well as local produce like fruit and vegetables.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There's also lots of pavement cafes where you can have a coffee and watch tourists from all over the world go by.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The oldest buildings in the area are on the main streets, including the city's first department store.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Built in the 1880s, which is still open today.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The Sheepmarket is a center for fashion, and there's a policy of encouraging new young designers.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The Young Fashion competition is open to local young people who are passionate about fashion.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This year, they've been asked to design an outfit based on ideas from the music and technology that's part of their everyday life, using both natural and man-made fibers.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The garments will be judged by a panel of experts and fashion designers, and the winning entries will be modeled at a special gala evening.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Parking at the Sheepmarket is easy.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There are plenty of pay and display car parking spaces on the roadsides, which are fine if you just want to stay for an hour or two.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But if you want to spend the day there, it's better to park in one of the four underground car parks.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's not expensive, and if you can present a receipt from one of the local stores, you'll not be charged at all.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: After 6 pm, many of the car parks have a flat rate which varies, but it is usually very reasonable.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the podcast, you have some time to look at questions 15 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 15 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: The Sheepmarket is one of the main centers for art and history in the whole of the country.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If you look at our map, you'll see some of the main attractions there.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Most visitors start from Crawley Road at the bottom of the map.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The Reynolds House is one of the oldest houses in the city, and is open to the public.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's on the north side of Crawley Road, next to the footpath that leads to the public gardens.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The area is particularly interesting for its unusual sculptures.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The Thumb's is just what its name suggests, but it's about 10 meters high.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You'll see it on Hill Road.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Across the road from the bank.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The museum's got a particularly fine collection of New Zealand landscapes.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's on the east side of the Sheepmarket on City Road.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's on the other side of the road from the public gardens, immediately facing the junction with Hill Road.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The contemporary art gallery is on a little road that leads off Station Square, not far from the public gardens.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The road ends at the gallery, it doesn't go anywhere else.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That's open every day except Mondays.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The Warner Gallery specializes in 19th century art.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's on City Road, near the junction with Crawley Road, on the same side of the road as the public gardens.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's open on weekdays from 9 to 5, and entry is free.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Finally, if you're interested in purchasing high quality artwork, the place to go is Nucleus.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You need to go from Crawley Road, up through Station Square, and east along Hill Road.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Until you get to a small winding road turning off.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Go up there, and it's on your right.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If you get to City Road, you've gone too far.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: The garments will be judged by a panel of experts and fashion designers, and the winning entries will be modeled at a special gala evening.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Parking at the Sheepmarket is easy.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There are plenty of pay and display car parking spaces on the roadsides, which are fine if you just want to stay for an hour or two.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But if you want to spend the day there, it's better to park in one of the four underground car parks.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's not expensive, and if you can present a receipt from one of the local stores, you'll not be charged at all.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: After 6 pm, many of the car parks have a flat rate which varies, but it is usually very reasonable.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Before you hear the rest of the podcast, you have some time to look at questions 15 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen and answer questions 15 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: The Sheepmarket is one of the main centers for art and history in the whole of the country.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: If you look at our map, you'll see some of the main attractions there.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Most visitors start from Crawley Road at the bottom of the map.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The Reynolds House is one of the oldest houses in the city, and is open to the public.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's on the north side of Crawley Road, next to the footpath that leads to the public gardens.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The area is particularly interesting for its unusual sculptures.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The Thumb's is just what its name suggests, but it's about 10 meters high.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: You'll see it on Hill Road.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Across the road from the bank.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The museum's got a particularly fine collection of New Zealand landscapes.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's on the east side of the Sheepmarket on City Road.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's on the other side of the road from the public gardens, immediately facing the junction with Hill Road.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The contemporary art gallery is on a little road that leads off Station Square, not far from the public gardens.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The road ends at the gallery, it doesn't go anywhere else.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That's open every day except Mondays.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The Warner Gallery specializes in 19th century art.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's on City Road, near the junction with Crawley Road, on the same side of the road as the public gardens.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's open on weekdays from 9 to 5, and entry is free.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Finally, if you're interested in purchasing high quality artwork, the place to go is Nucleus.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: You need to go from Crawley Road, up through Station Square, and east along Hill Road.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Until you get to a small winding road turning off.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Go up there, and it's on your right.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: If you get to City Road, you've gone too far.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: Which is the most rapidly-growing group of residents in the Sheepmarket area?
The speaker recommends the side streets in the Sheepmarket for their
Clothes designed by entrants for the Young Fashion competition must
options: be modelled by the designers themselves.
be inspired by aspects of contemporary culture.
be made from locally produced materials.

items.title: Car parking is free in some car parks if you
options: stay for less than an hour.
park in the evenings or at weekends.

audioscript.text: Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Will hear two students, Katie and Joe discussing a presentation in their Film Studies course.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully, and answer questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Joe, you know I'm giving a presentation in our film studies class next week.
audioscript.speaker: KATIE

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: Well, could we discuss it?
audioscript.speaker: KATIE

audioscript.text: I could do with getting someone else's opinion.
audioscript.speaker: KATIE

audioscript.text: Of course, Katie.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: What are you going to talk about?
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: It's about film adaptations of Shakespeare's plays.
audioscript.speaker: KATIE

audioscript.text: I've got very interested in all the different approaches that film directors take.
audioscript.speaker: KATIE

audioscript.text: Uh huh.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: So I thought I'd start with Giannetti, who's a professor of film and literature, and in one of his books, he came up with a straightforward classification of film adaptations based on how faithful they are to the original plays and novels.
audioscript.speaker: KATIE

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: I've already made some notes on that, so I just need to sort those out before the presentation.
audioscript.speaker: KATIE

audioscript.text: I thought that next, I'd ask the class to come up with the worst examples of Shakespeare adaptations that they've seen.
audioscript.speaker: KATIE

audioscript.text: And to say why, that should be more fun than having their favorite versions.
audioscript.speaker: KATIE

audioscript.text: Yes, I can certainly think of a couple.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: Hmm.
audioscript.speaker: KATIE

audioscript.text: Right, next, I want to talk about Rachel Malchow.
audioscript.speaker: KATIE

audioscript.text: I came across something on the internet about her work on film adaptations.
audioscript.speaker: KATIE

audioscript.text: And I was thinking of showing some film clips to illustrate her ideas.
audioscript.speaker: KATIE

audioscript.text: Will you have enough time though, both to prepare and during the presentation?
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: After all, I doubt if you'll be able to find all the clips you want.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: Hmm. Perhaps you're right.
audioscript.speaker: KATIE

audioscript.text: OK, well, I'd better do some slides instead.
audioscript.speaker: KATIE

audioscript.text: Saying how various films relate to what she says, that should encourage discussion.
audioscript.speaker: KATIE

audioscript.text: Hmm.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: Next, I want to say something about how plays may be chosen for adaptation.
audioscript.speaker: KATIE

audioscript.text: Because they're concerned with issues of the time when the film is made.
audioscript.speaker: KATIE

audioscript.text: You mean things like patriotism, or the role of governments?
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: Exactly.
audioscript.speaker: KATIE

audioscript.text: It's quite tricky, but I've got a few ideas I'd like to discuss.
audioscript.speaker: KATIE

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 25 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: I listen and answer questions 25 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: And finally, I want to talk about a few adaptations that I think illustrate a range of approaches, and make some comments on them.
audioscript.speaker: KATIE

audioscript.text: Do you know the Japanese film Ran?
audioscript.speaker: KATIE

audioscript.text: I haven't seen it.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: It was based on Shakespeare's King Lear, wasn't it?
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: KATIE

audioscript.text: It was a very loose adaptation, using the same situation and story, but moving it to 16th century Japan.
audioscript.speaker: KATIE

audioscript.text: Instead of 16th century Britain.
audioscript.speaker: KATIE

audioscript.text: So, for example, the king's daughters become sons, because in Japanese culture at that time women couldn't succeed to the throne.
audioscript.speaker: KATIE

audioscript.text: OK, I hope you're going to talk about the 1993 film of Much Ado About Nothing.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: I think that's one of the best Shakespeare films.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: It really brings the play to life, doesn't it?
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: Yes, I agree.
audioscript.speaker: KATIE

audioscript.text: And I think filming it in Italy, where the play is set, makes you see what life was like at the time of the play.
audioscript.speaker: KATIE

audioscript.text: Absolutely, right, what's next?
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: Uh. Next, I thought Romeo and Juliet, the 1996 film, which moves the action into the present day.
audioscript.speaker: KATIE

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: It worked really well, I thought, changing the two feuding families in the original to two competing business empires, even though they're speaking in the English of the original play.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: You'd expect it would sound really bizarre, but I found I soon got used to it.
audioscript.speaker: KATIE

audioscript.text: Me too.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: Then I thought I'd include a real Hollywood film.
audioscript.speaker: KATIE

audioscript.text: One that's intended to appeal to a mass commercial audience.
audioscript.speaker: KATIE

audioscript.text: There must be quite a number of those.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: Yes, but I've picked the 1996 film of Hamlet.
audioscript.speaker: KATIE

audioscript.text: It included every line of the text, but it's more like a typical action hero movie.
audioscript.speaker: KATIE

audioscript.text: There are loads of special effects, but no unifying interpretation of the play.
audioscript.speaker: KATIE

audioscript.text: All show and no substance.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: Exactly.
audioscript.speaker: KATIE

audioscript.text: Then there's Prospero's Books, based on The Tempest. That was really innovative from a stylistic point of view.
audioscript.speaker: KATIE

audioscript.text: Didn't it include dance and singing and animation as well as live actors?
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: Yes, it did.
audioscript.speaker: KATIE

audioscript.text: I also want to mention Looking for Richard.
audioscript.speaker: KATIE

audioscript.text: Did you ever see it?
audioscript.speaker: KATIE

audioscript.text: No, but I've read about it.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: It was a blend of a documentary with a few scenes from Richard III, wasn't it?
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: KATIE

audioscript.text: It's more a way of looking into how people nowadays connect with the playwright.
audioscript.speaker: KATIE

audioscript.text: The play is really just the starting point, and that'll be where I finish.
audioscript.speaker: KATIE

audioscript.text: Well, it sounds as though it'll be very interesting.
audioscript.speaker: JOE

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items: Introduce Giannetti's book containing a <input> of adaptations

Discuss relationship between adaptations and <input> at the time of making the film
body.title: Presentation on film adaptations of Shakespeare's plays

text: What do the speakers say about each of the following films?
textReadable: What do the speakers say about each of the following films?

list: contains only parts of the play
is too similar to another kind of film
turned out to be unpopular with audiences
presents the play in a different period from the original
sets the original in a different country
incorporates a variety of art forms
body.title: Films

audioscript.text: Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear part of a lecture about noise in cities.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: This lecture will be about the science of acoustics, the study of sound in relation to urban environments such as cities.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As an acoustic engineer myself I think this is an area where we're likely to see great changes.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In the past, researching urban soundscapes was simple.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We measured levels of sound in decibels.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So I used to take my sound meter, and I measured the noise somewhere.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And then I might ask a sample of people to say at what level the sound became annoying.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: With data like this, acoustic engineers have been able to build up what we call noise maps, maps of the sound environment, but actually these aren't a lot of use.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: What they do show is that the highest noise levels are generally on roads.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Well, that's not really very surprising.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But there's quite a lot going on that these maps don't show, because they can't capture the complex way that sound varies over time.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So they ignore important issues such as the noise someone might hear from the open windows or gardens of their neighbors, and this sort of noise can be quite significant in summer.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We don't have any databases on this sort of information.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As well as that, these records of sound levels take no account of the fact that people vary in their perceptions of noise.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So someone like me with years of working in acoustics might be very different from you in that regard, but anyway, even though these noise maps are fairly crude, they've been useful in providing information and raising awareness that noise matters.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We need to deal with it, and so it's a political matter.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And that's important.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We need rules and regulations, because noise can cause all sorts of problems.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Those of you who are city dwellers know that things go on 24 hours a day, so city dwellers often suffer from interrupted sleep.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's also known that noise can lead to a rise in levels of stress.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Due to physical changes in the body, affecting the composition of the blood.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And there are other problems as well, for instance, if school children don't have a quiet place to study.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Their work will suffer.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now one problem with decibel measurement is that it doesn't differentiate between different types of noise.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Some types of sounds that most people would probably think of as nice and relaxing.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Might well score quite highly in decibel levels.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Think of the sound made by a fountain in a town square, for example.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That's not necessarily something that we'd want to control or reduce.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So maybe researchers should consider these sorts of sounds in urban design.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This is going to be tricky, because just measuring decibel levels isn't going to help us here.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Instead, many researchers are using social science techniques, studying people's emotional response to sound by using questionnaires and so on.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So what exactly do people want to hear in an urban environment?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Some recent interdisciplinary research has come out with results that at first sight seem contradictory.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A city needs to have a sense of activity, so it needs to be lively, with sounds like the clack of high heels on a pavement, or the hiss of a coffee machine.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But these mustn't be too intrusive, because at the same time, we need to be able to relax.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One of the major problems in achieving this will be getting architects and town planners to use the research, apart from studying the basics of acoustics.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These people receive very little training in this area, but in fact, they should be regarding sound as an opportunity to add to the experience of urban living.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Whereas at present, they tend to see it as something to be avoided or reduced as far as possible, or something that's just a job for engineers, like the street drainage system.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: What's needed is for noise in cities to be regarded as an aesthetic quality, as something that has the qualities of an art form.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If we acknowledge this, then we urgently need to know what governs it, and how designers can work with it.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We need to develop a complex understanding of many factors.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: What is the relationship between sound and culture?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: What can we learn from disciplines such as psychology about the way that sound interacts with human development and social relationships?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And the way that sound affects our thoughts and feelings.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Can we learn anything from physics about the nature of sound itself?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Today's powerful technologies can also help us. To show us their ideas, and help us to imagine the effect their buildings will have, architects and town planners already use virtual reality - but these programs are silent.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In the future, such programs could use realistic sounds, meaning that soundscapes could be explored before being built.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So hopefully, using the best technology we can lay our hands on, the city of the future will be a pleasure to the ears as well as the eyes.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You will hear part of a lecture about noise in cities.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: This lecture will be about the science of acoustics, the study of sound in relation to urban environments such as cities.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As an acoustic engineer myself I think this is an area where we're likely to see great changes.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In the past, researching urban soundscapes was simple.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We measured levels of sound in decibels.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So I used to take my sound meter, and I measured the noise somewhere.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And then I might ask a sample of people to say at what level the sound became annoying.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: With data like this, acoustic engineers have been able to build up what we call noise maps, maps of the sound environment, but actually these aren't a lot of use.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: What they do show is that the highest noise levels are generally on roads.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Well, that's not really very surprising.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But there's quite a lot going on that these maps don't show, because they can't capture the complex way that sound varies over time.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So they ignore important issues such as the noise someone might hear from the open windows or gardens of their neighbors, and this sort of noise can be quite significant in summer.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We don't have any databases on this sort of information.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As well as that, these records of sound levels take no account of the fact that people vary in their perceptions of noise.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So someone like me with years of working in acoustics might be very different from you in that regard, but anyway, even though these noise maps are fairly crude, they've been useful in providing information and raising awareness that noise matters.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We need to deal with it, and so it's a political matter.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And that's important.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We need rules and regulations, because noise can cause all sorts of problems.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Those of you who are city dwellers know that things go on 24 hours a day, so city dwellers often suffer from interrupted sleep.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's also known that noise can lead to a rise in levels of stress.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Due to physical changes in the body, affecting the composition of the blood.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And there are other problems as well, for instance, if school children don't have a quiet place to study.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Their work will suffer.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Now one problem with decibel measurement is that it doesn't differentiate between different types of noise.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Some types of sounds that most people would probably think of as nice and relaxing.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Might well score quite highly in decibel levels.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Think of the sound made by a fountain in a town square, for example.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That's not necessarily something that we'd want to control or reduce.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So maybe researchers should consider these sorts of sounds in urban design.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This is going to be tricky, because just measuring decibel levels isn't going to help us here.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Instead, many researchers are using social science techniques, studying people's emotional response to sound by using questionnaires and so on.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So what exactly do people want to hear in an urban environment?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Some recent interdisciplinary research has come out with results that at first sight seem contradictory.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: A city needs to have a sense of activity, so it needs to be lively, with sounds like the clack of high heels on a pavement, or the hiss of a coffee machine.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But these mustn't be too intrusive, because at the same time, we need to be able to relax.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: One of the major problems in achieving this will be getting architects and town planners to use the research, apart from studying the basics of acoustics.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: These people receive very little training in this area, but in fact, they should be regarding sound as an opportunity to add to the experience of urban living.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Whereas at present, they tend to see it as something to be avoided or reduced as far as possible, or something that's just a job for engineers, like the street drainage system.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: What's needed is for noise in cities to be regarded as an aesthetic quality, as something that has the qualities of an art form.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: If we acknowledge this, then we urgently need to know what governs it, and how designers can work with it.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We need to develop a complex understanding of many factors.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: What is the relationship between sound and culture?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: What can we learn from disciplines such as psychology about the way that sound interacts with human development and social relationships?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And the way that sound affects our thoughts and feelings.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Can we learn anything from physics about the nature of sound itself?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Today's powerful technologies can also help us. To show us their ideas, and help us to imagine the effect their buildings will have, architects and town planners already use virtual reality - but these programs are silent.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In the future, such programs could use realistic sounds, meaning that soundscapes could be explored before being built.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So hopefully, using the best technology we can lay our hands on, the city of the future will be a pleasure to the ears as well as the eyes.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of Section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

do not get much <input> in acoustics

items.title: Understanding sound as an art form

body.title: Noise in Cities
